infobox.key: [vampire, face of little green man, feather pen (quill) and fire-breathing dragon – to the right of that are scripted words "Speculative (over) Fiction"](/wiki/File:Speculative_fiction_portal_logo_small_transparent.png)

infobox.value: | * [v](/wiki/Template:Speculative_fiction_sidebar "Template:Speculative fiction sidebar") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Speculative_fiction_sidebar "Template talk:Speculative fiction sidebar") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Speculative_fiction_sidebar "Special:EditPage/Template:Speculative fiction sidebar")

is a [film genre](/wiki/Film_genre "Film genre") that seeks to elicit physical or psychological [fear](/wiki/Fear "Fear") in its viewers.[[2]](#cite_note-Kuhn-2012-2) Horror films often explore dark subject matter and may deal with [transgressive topics or themes](/wiki/Transgressive_art "Transgressive art"). Broad elements of the genre include [monsters](/wiki/Monster_movie "Monster movie"), [apocalyptic events](/wiki/Apocalyptic_and_post-apocalyptic_fiction "Apocalyptic and post-apocalyptic fiction"), and [religious](/wiki/Religion "Religion") or [folk](/wiki/Folk_horror "Folk horror") beliefs.

[Max Schreck](/wiki/Max_Schreck "Max Schreck") as [Count Orlok](/wiki/Count_Orlok "Count Orlok") in the 1922 film *[Nosferatu](/wiki/Nosferatu "Nosferatu")*. **Horror** is a [film genre](/wiki/Film_genre "Film genre") that seeks to elicit physical or psychological [fear](/wiki/Fear "Fear") in its viewers.[[2]](#cite_note-Kuhn-2012-2) Horror films often explore dark subject matter and may deal with [transgressive topics or themes](/wiki/Transgressive_art "Transgressive art"). Broad elements of the genre include [monsters](/wiki/Monster_movie "Monster movie"), [apocalyptic events](/wiki/Apocalyptic_and_post-apocalyptic_fiction "Apocalyptic and post-apocalyptic fiction"), and [religious](/wiki/Religion "Religion") or [folk](/wiki/Folk_horror "Folk horror") beliefs.

Horror films have existed [since the early 20th century](/wiki/History_of_horror_films "History of horror films"). Early inspirations predating film include [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore"); the religious beliefs and [superstitions](/wiki/Superstition "Superstition") of different cultures; and the [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_fiction "Gothic fiction") and [horror literature](/wiki/Horror_fiction "Horror fiction") of authors such as [Edgar Allan Poe](/wiki/Edgar_Allan_Poe "Edgar Allan Poe"), [Bram Stoker](/wiki/Bram_Stoker "Bram Stoker"), and [Mary Shelley](/wiki/Mary_Shelley "Mary Shelley"). From its origins in [silent films](/wiki/Silent_film "Silent film") and [German Expressionism](/wiki/German_expressionist_cinema "German expressionist cinema"), horror became a codified [genre](/wiki/Genre "Genre") only after the release of [*Dracula*](/wiki/Dracula_(1931_English-language_film) "Dracula (1931 English-language film)") (1931). Many sub-genres emerged in subsequent decades, including [body horror](/wiki/Body_horror "Body horror"), [comedy horror](/wiki/Comedy_horror "Comedy horror"), [erotic horror](/wiki/Erotic_horror "Erotic horror"), [slasher films](/wiki/Slasher_film "Slasher film"), [splatter films](/wiki/Splatter_film "Splatter film"), [supernatural horror](/wiki/Supernatural_horror_film "Supernatural horror film"), and [psychological horror](/wiki/Psychological_horror_(film_and_television) "Psychological horror (film and television)"). The genre has been produced worldwide, varying in content and style between regions. Horror is particularly prominent in the cinema of [Japan](/wiki/Japanese_horror "Japanese horror"), [Korea](/wiki/Korean_horror "Korean horror"), and [Thailand](/wiki/Thai_horror "Thai horror"), among other countries.

Despite being the subject of social and legal controversy due to their subject matter, some horror films and franchises have seen major [commercial success](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_horror_films "List of highest-grossing horror films"), influenced society, and generated popular [culture icons](/wiki/Cultural_icon "Cultural icon").

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

See also: [Horror and terror](/wiki/Horror_and_terror "Horror and terror")

The book *The Film Experience: An Introduction* (2021) defines the horror film as a genre with origins in Gothic literature that seeks to frighten the viewer. The authors highlight the fundamental elements of the horror film as "characters with physical, psychological, or spiritual deformities"; "narratives built on suspense, surprise, and shock"; and "visual compositions that move between the dread of not seeing and the horror of seeing".[[2]](#cite_note-Kuhn-2012-2)

Alternatively*, The Dictionary of Film Studies* (2012) defines the horror film as representing "disturbing and dark subject matter, seeking to elicit responses of [fear](/wiki/Fear "Fear"), [terror](/wiki/Horror_and_terror "Horror and terror"), [disgust](/wiki/Disgust "Disgust"), shock, [suspense](/wiki/Suspense "Suspense"), and (of course) horror from their viewers."[[3]](#cite_note-3) In his chapter "The American Nightmare: Horror in the 70s" from the book *Hollywood from Vietnam to Reagan* (2002), film critic [Robin Wood](/wiki/Robin_Wood_(critic) "Robin Wood (critic)") declared that the commonality between horror films is that “normality is threatened by the monster.”[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBalmain20084-4) This idea was further expanded on by [Noël Carroll](/wiki/No%C3%ABl_Carroll "Noël Carroll") in his book *The Philosophy of Horror, or Paradoxes of the Heart* (1990); he noted that "repulsion must be pleasurable, as evidenced by the genre's popularity."[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBalmain20084-4)

Before the release of *[Dracula](/wiki/Dracula_(1931_English-language_film) "Dracula (1931 English-language film)")* (1931), as historian [Gary D. Rhodes](/wiki/Gary_D._Rhodes "Gary D. Rhodes") explained, the ideas and terminology of horror films did not yet exist as a codified [genre](/wiki/Film_genre "Film genre"), although critics had used the term *horror* to describe films in reviews prior to *Dracula*'s release.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes201491-5) *Horror* was a term used with a variety of meanings. In 1913, the magazine *[Moving Picture World](/wiki/The_Moving_Picture_World "The Moving Picture World")* defined *horrors* as showcasing "striped convicts, murderous Indians, grinning 'black-handers', homicidal drunkards".[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes2018b97-6) Some titles directly suggested horror, such as *The Hand of Horror* (1914), a melodrama about a thief who steals from his own sister.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes2018b97-6) During the silent era, the term *horror* was used to describe everything from battle scenes in war films to stories of drug addiction.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes2018b98-7) Rhodes concluded that the term *horror film* (or *horror movie*) was not used in early cinema.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes2018b97-98-8)

The [mystery film](/wiki/Mystery_film "Mystery film") genre was then in vogue, and early information commonly promoted *Dracula* as a mystery film, despite the [novel](/wiki/Dracula "Dracula"), the [play](/wiki/Dracula_(1924_play) "Dracula (1924 play)"), and the film relying on the [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural").[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes201490-9) [Kim Newman](/wiki/Kim_Newman "Kim Newman") discussed the genre in [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute")'s book *Companion to Horror*, where he noted that horror films in the 1930s were easy to identify, but after that decade, “the more blurred distinctions become, and horror becomes less like a discrete genre than an effect which can be deployed within any number of narrative settings or narrative patterns”.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBalmain20085-10) In the 1940s, the horror film was viewed in different terms. Critic [Siegfried Kracauer](/wiki/Siegfried_Kracauer "Siegfried Kracauer") included *[The Lost Weekend](/wiki/The_Lost_Weekend "The Lost Weekend")* among films described as “terror films,” along with *[Shadow of a Doubt](/wiki/Shadow_of_a_Doubt "Shadow of a Doubt")* (1943), *[The Dark Corner](/wiki/The_Dark_Corner "The Dark Corner")* (1946), *[Gaslight](/wiki/Gaslight_(1944_film) "Gaslight (1944 film)")* (1944), *[Shock](/wiki/Shock_(1946_film) "Shock (1946 film)")* (1946), *[The Spiral Staircase](/wiki/The_Spiral_Staircase_(1946_film) "The Spiral Staircase (1946 film)")* (1946), *[The Stranger](/wiki/The_Stranger_(1946_film) "The Stranger (1946 film)")* (1946), and *[Spellbound](/wiki/Spellbound_(1945_film) "Spellbound (1945 film)")* (1945). Two years earlier, the *[New York Times](/wiki/New_York_Times "New York Times")* described a new cycle of “horror” productions included *Gaslight*, *[The Woman in the Window](/wiki/The_Woman_in_the_Window_(1944_film) "The Woman in the Window (1944 film)")* (1944), *[Dark Waters](/wiki/Dark_Waters_(1944_film) "Dark Waters (1944 film)")* (1944), *[Laura](/wiki/Laura_(1944_film) "Laura (1944 film)") (1944),* and *[Phantom Lady](/wiki/Phantom_Lady_(film) "Phantom Lady (film)")* (1944).[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJancovich200828-11) Mark Jancovich wrote in the book *The Shifting Definitions of Genre: Essays on Labeling Films, Television Shows and Media* (2008) that *horror* was virtually synonymous with mystery as a generic term, not being limited to films concerned with the strange, eerie, and uncanny.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJancovich200828-11)

Various writings on genre from Altman, Lawrence Alloway (*Violent America: The Movies 1946-1964* (1971)) and Peter Hutchings (*Approaches to Popular Film* (1995)) implied that it is easier to view films as [cycles](/wiki/Social_cycle_theory "Social cycle theory") rather than genres, suggesting that viewing the [slasher film](/wiki/Slasher_film "Slasher film") as a cycle would locate it in terms of several factors: the film industry's economy and production, the personnel involved in particular eras, and the manner of film marketing, distribution, and exhibition.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJancovich200031-32-12)
In an essay, Mark Jancovich declared that "there is no simple 'collective belief' as to what constitutes the horror genre" among [fans](/wiki/Fandom "Fandom") and [critics](/wiki/Film_criticism "Film criticism") of the genre.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJancovich200025-26-13) Jancovich found that disagreements existed between audiences who wanted to distinguish themselves. Such disagreement included fans of other genres who may view a film such as *[Alien](/wiki/Alien_(film) "Alien (film)")* (1979) as belonging to [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film"), as well as horror fans dismissing it as inauthentic for either genre.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJancovich200026-27-14) Further debates exist among genre fans having personal definitions of “true” horror films: some fans embrace [cult figures](/wiki/Cult_following "Cult following") such as [Freddy Krueger](/wiki/Freddy_Krueger "Freddy Krueger") of the *[A Nightmare on Elm Street](/wiki/A_Nightmare_on_Elm_Street_(franchise) "A Nightmare on Elm Street (franchise)")* series, while other fans disassociate themselves from characters and series and focus instead on genre *[auteur](/wiki/Auteur "Auteur")* directors such as [Dario Argento](/wiki/Dario_Argento "Dario Argento"), while still other fans would deem Argento's films too mainstream, preferring more [underground films](/wiki/Underground_film "Underground film").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJancovich200028-15) Andrew Tudor wrote in his book *Monsters and Mad Scientists: A Cultural History of the Horror Movie* that "Genre is what we collectively believe it to be."[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETudor19916-7-16)

### Cinematic techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cinematic techniques")]

Jacob Shelton investigated in detail the many ways that horror films manipulate audience members.[[17]](#cite_note-Ranker-17) Shelton notes that [negative space](/wiki/Negative_space "Negative space") can draw viewers' eyes to anything in the frame—for example, a wall or the empty blackness in the shadows.[[17]](#cite_note-Ranker-17)

Anna Powell explores how horror directors use [cinematography](/wiki/Cinematography "Cinematography") to induce certain viewer reactions. Powell observes that lighting extremes, whether bright or dark, can prevent viewers from seeing every detail in a scene, which provokes unease. Bright lighting might also trick viewers into feeling safe.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPowell2005120-18)

Powell also points out how distorting space and time can confuse and disorient horror film viewers.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPowell2005130-19) To confound viewers' senses, directors might use [tilted camera angles](/wiki/Dutch_angle "Dutch angle") and shots in slow-motion or reverse.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPowell2005131-2-20)

Powell notes that directors also use colour, through costuming, setting, and [lens filters](/wiki/Photographic_filter "Photographic filter"), to communicate mood and evoke certain connotations. Red, for example, might convey blood, passion, or disease.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPowell2005137-21) Contrasting colours bring viewers’ attention to certain places in the frame.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPowell2005141-22)

The [jump scare](/wiki/Jump_scare "Jump scare") is a horror film [trope](/wiki/Trope_(cinema) "Trope (cinema)") where an abrupt change in image accompanied by a loud sound aims to surprise the viewer.[[17]](#cite_note-Ranker-17) This trope can also be [subverted](/wiki/Subversion "Subversion") to create tension, as audiences may feel increased unease and discomfort in anticipating jump scares.[[17]](#cite_note-Ranker-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Woman-wearing-red-t-shirt-2898511.jpg/250px-Woman-wearing-red-t-shirt-2898511.jpg)](/wiki/File:Woman-wearing-red-t-shirt-2898511.jpg)

Mirrors are often used to create a sense of tension in horror films.

[Mirrors](/wiki/Mirror "Mirror") are often used in horror films to create visual depth and build tension. Shelton argues that mirrors have been used so often in horror films that audiences have been [conditioned](/wiki/Classical_conditioning "Classical conditioning") to fear them, and subverting audience expectations of a jump scare in a mirror can further build tension.[[17]](#cite_note-Ranker-17) [Tight framing](/wiki/Framing_(visual_arts) "Framing (visual arts)") and [close-ups](/wiki/Close-up "Close-up") are also commonly used; these can build tension and induce [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety") by not allowing the viewer to see beyond what is near the [protagonist](/wiki/Protagonist "Protagonist").[[17]](#cite_note-Ranker-17)

The interaction between horror films and their audiences is another significant issue discussed by Rhodes. He notes that horror films often serve as a safe space for viewers to confront and process their fears. This cathartic experience can provide psychological relief and a sense of empowerment, as viewers face and overcome their anxieties in a controlled environment. The communal experience of watching horror films in theaters or discussing them in fan communities also plays a crucial role in the genre's impact and popularity.[6]

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [Horror film score](/wiki/Horror_film_score "Horror film score")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/John_Carpenter_Live_2016.jpg/250px-John_Carpenter_Live_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Carpenter_Live_2016.jpg)

Filmmaker and composer [John Carpenter](/wiki/John_Carpenter "John Carpenter"), who has directed and scored [numerous horror films](/wiki/John_Carpenter_filmography "John Carpenter filmography"), performing in 2016

[Music](/wiki/Horror_film_score "Horror film score") is a key element of horror films. In his book *Music in the Horror Film* (2010), Lerner writes that "music in horror film frequently makes us feel threatened and uncomfortable" and intends to intensify the atmosphere created by imagery and themes. Music helps to set the tense or chilling mood that horror movies often aim to achieve; it can even cause physiological effects that override learned reactions and behaviors.[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Dissonance](/wiki/Dissonance_(music) "Dissonance (music)"), [atonality](/wiki/Atonality "Atonality") and experiments with [timbre](/wiki/Timbre "Timbre") are typical techniques used by [composers](/wiki/Composer "Composer") in horror film music.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Themes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Themes")]

[Frankenstein's monster](/wiki/Frankenstein%27s_monster "Frankenstein's monster")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Apocalypse-Albert_Goodwin.jpg/250px-Apocalypse-Albert_Goodwin.jpg)](/wiki/Apocalyptic_and_post-apocalyptic_fiction "Apocalyptic and post-apocalyptic fiction")

*Apocalypse* by [Albert Goodwin](/wiki/Albert_Goodwin_(artist) "Albert Goodwin (artist)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Kitab_al-Bulhan_--_devil.jpg/120px-Kitab_al-Bulhan_--_devil.jpg)](/wiki/Demon "Demon")

A [demon](/wiki/Demon "Demon") in the [Book of Wonders](/wiki/Book_of_Wonders "Book of Wonders")

Charles Derry proposed that the three key components of horror are personality, Armageddon and the demonic.

In his book *Dark Dreams*, author Charles Derry conceived horror films as focusing on three broad [themes](/wiki/Theme_(narrative) "Theme (narrative)"): the horror of [personality](/wiki/Personality "Personality"), the horror of [Armageddon](/wiki/Armageddon "Armageddon"), and the horror of the [demonic](/wiki/Demon "Demon").[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrawer198916-25) First, the horror of personality derives from [monsters](/wiki/Monster_movie "Monster movie") being at the centre of the plot, such as [Frankenstein's monster](/wiki/Frankenstein%27s_monster "Frankenstein's monster"), whose psychology makes them perform horrific acts including [rapes](/wiki/Rape "Rape"), [mutilations](/wiki/Mutilation "Mutilation"), and [sadistic](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sadistic "wikt:sadistic") killings.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrawer198916-25) Other key works with this theme include [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock")'s *[Psycho](/wiki/Psycho_(1960_film) "Psycho (1960 film)"),* which feature psychotic murderers without monster make-up.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrawer198916-25) Second, the Armageddon theme explores the fear of [large-scale destruction](/wiki/Apocalyptic_and_post-apocalyptic_fiction "Apocalyptic and post-apocalyptic fiction"), which includes [science fiction works](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_horror_films "List of science fiction horror films") but also [natural events](/wiki/List_of_natural_horror_films "List of natural horror films"), such as Hitchcock's *[The Birds](/wiki/The_Birds_(film) "The Birds (film)")* (1963).[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrawer198916-25) Finally, the theme of the demonic features graphic accounts of [satanic rites](/wiki/Satanic_film "Satanic film"), [witchcraft](/wiki/Witchcraft "Witchcraft"), and [exorcisms](/wiki/Exorcism "Exorcism") outside traditional forms of worship, as seen in films such as *[The Exorcist](/wiki/The_Exorcist "The Exorcist")* (1973) and *[The Omen](/wiki/The_Omen "The Omen")* (1976).[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrawer198917-26)

Some critics have suggested that horror films can be vessels for exploring contemporary cultural, political and social trends. Jeanne Hall, a film theorist, supports the use of horror films to ease the process of understanding issues by making use of their visual elements.[[27]](#cite_note-Lizardi-27) The use of horror films can help audiences to understand prior historical events, for example, the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), the [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust"), the worldwide [AIDS epidemic](/wiki/AIDS_epidemic "AIDS epidemic")[[28]](#cite_note-History_Horror-28) or [post-9/11](/wiki/Post-9/11 "Post-9/11") pessimism.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAstonWalliss20134-29)

Anxieties surrounding race and racism have historically and continuously informed the horror genre.[[2]](#cite_note-Kuhn-2012-2) A good example is the history of the [zombie apocalypse](/wiki/Zombie_apocalypse "Zombie apocalypse") subgenre. The first [zombie](/wiki/Zombie "Zombie") horror films, such as *[White Zombie](/wiki/White_Zombie_(film) "White Zombie (film)")* (1932), were inspired by stories brought back to Europe by colonizers, and these stories explicitly presented [Afro-Haitian](/wiki/Afro-Haitians "Afro-Haitians") religious and spiritual practices as evil and perverse.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The film which later revived the subgenre, *[Night of the Living Dead](/wiki/Night_of_the_Living_Dead "Night of the Living Dead")* (1968), incidentally presented themes surrounding race in America by casting [Duane Jones](/wiki/Duane_Jones "Duane Jones"), a Black actor, as the lead.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Whether accidentally or actively, horror films demonstrate societal issues by who or what is chosen to incite fear, and how this choice is represented visually and narratively.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Devils_castle_poster.jpg/250px-Devils_castle_poster.jpg)](/wiki/File:Devils_castle_poster.jpg)

Poster for *[Le Manoir du diable](/wiki/Le_Manoir_du_diable "Le Manoir du diable")* (1896), sometimes described as the first horror film

The [history of horror films](/wiki/History_of_horror_films "History of horror films") was described by author [Siegbert Solomon Prawer](/wiki/Siegbert_Salomon_Prawer "Siegbert Salomon Prawer") as difficult to read as a linear path, with the genre changing through the decades, based on the [state of cinema](/wiki/History_of_film "History of film"), [audience tastes](/wiki/Audience_theory "Audience theory") and [contemporary world events](/wiki/History "History").

Films before the [1930s](/wiki/List_of_horror_films_of_the_1930s "List of horror films of the 1930s"), such as early [German expressionist cinema](/wiki/German_expressionist_cinema "German expressionist cinema") and [trick films](/wiki/Trick_film "Trick film"), have been retrospectively described as horror films, since the genre did not become codified until the release of *[Dracula](/wiki/Dracula_(1931_English-language_film) "Dracula (1931 English-language film)")* (1931). *Dracula* was a box office success, leading [Universal](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures") and several other American film studios to develop and popularise horror films well into the [1940s](/wiki/List_of_horror_films_of_the_1940s "List of horror films of the 1940s"). By [the 1950s](/wiki/List_of_horror_films_of_the_1950s "List of horror films of the 1950s"), horror was often made with [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction_horror "Science fiction horror") themes, and towards the end of the decade horror was a more common genre of international productions.

The [1960s](/wiki/List_of_horror_films_of_the_1960s "List of horror films of the 1960s") saw further developments, with material based on contemporary works instead of classic literature. The release of films such as *[Psycho](/wiki/Psycho_(1960_film) "Psycho (1960 film)"),* *[Black Sunday](/wiki/Black_Sunday_(1960_film) "Black Sunday (1960 film)")* and *[Night of the Living Dead](/wiki/Night_of_the_Living_Dead "Night of the Living Dead")* led to an increase in violence and erotic scenes within the genre. The [1970s](/wiki/List_of_horror_films_of_the_1970s "List of horror films of the 1970s") would expand on these themes with films that would delve into gorier pictures, as well as films that were near or direct [pornographic](/wiki/Pornographic_film "Pornographic film") hybrids. Genre cycles in this era include the [natural horror film](/wiki/Natural_horror_film "Natural horror film"), and the rise of [slasher films](/wiki/Slasher_film "Slasher film") which expanded in the early [1980s](/wiki/List_of_horror_films_of_the_1980s "List of horror films of the 1980s"). Towards the 1990s, [postmodernism](/wiki/Postmodern_horror "Postmodern horror") entered horror, while some of the biggest hits of the decade included [films from Japan](/wiki/Japanese_horror "Japanese horror") such as the successful *[Ring](/wiki/Ring_(1998_film) "Ring (1998 film)")* (1998).

In the 21st century, [streaming media](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") popularised horror trends. These trends included [torture porn](/wiki/Torture_porn "Torture porn") influenced by the success of *[Saw](/wiki/Saw_(2004_film) "Saw (2004 film)")*; films using a "[found footage](/wiki/Found_footage_(film_technique) "Found footage (film technique)")" technique; and independent productions such as *[Get Out](/wiki/Get_Out "Get Out")*, [*Hereditary*](/wiki/Hereditary_(film) "Hereditary (film)"), and the *[Insidious](/wiki/Insidious_(film_series) "Insidious (film series)")* series which were box office hits.

In discussing narrative trends in horror, scholar Isabel Pinedo draws on the book *Monsters and Mad Scientists: A Cultural History of the Horror Movie* (1989) to map the development of the Anglo-American horror genre. She points out that early synchronized sound, as well as post-war period films, present threats to social order as external, while human agency (largely male) prevails. The focus is on the monster's acts of violence and the characters' failed attempts at resistance, ending with male experts using violence or knowledge to defeat the monster and restore social order.[[32]](#cite_note-:0-32)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETudor1991-33)
Pinedo presents the category of postmodern horror. She summarizes the narrative structure as indicating the prevalence of internal threats to social order by using a combination of monstrous violence and ineffectual human resistance with open endings. These open endings may be as follows: the monster triumphs as in *[Henry: Portrait of a Serial Killer](/wiki/Henry:_Portrait_of_a_Serial_Killer "Henry: Portrait of a Serial Killer")* (1990); the monster is defeated but only temporarily as in *[Halloween](/wiki/Halloween_(1978_film) "Halloween (1978 film)")* (1978); or there is an ambiguous outcome as in *[Night of the Living Dead](/wiki/Night_of_the_Living_Dead "Night of the Living Dead")* (1968), *[The Texas Chainsaw Massacre](/wiki/The_Texas_Chain_Saw_Massacre "The Texas Chain Saw Massacre")* (1974), *[The Thing](/wiki/The_Thing_(1982_film) "The Thing (1982 film)")* (1982), and *[Nightmare on Elm Street](/wiki/A_Nightmare_on_Elm_Street "A Nightmare on Elm Street")* (1984).[[32]](#cite_note-:0-32)

## Sub-genres of horror film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Sub-genres of horror film")]

Horror is a malleable genre, and it can often be altered to accommodate other genres such as [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film"), which makes some films difficult to categorize.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOdellLe_Blanc200715-34)

### Lovecraftian horror

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Lovecraftian horror")]

Main article: [Lovecraftian horror](/wiki/Lovecraftian_horror "Lovecraftian horror")

Named after American author [H. P. Lovecraft](/wiki/H._P._Lovecraft "H. P. Lovecraft") (1890–1937), this sub-genre of horror films include themes of [cosmic dread](/wiki/Existential_dread "Existential dread"), forbidden and dangerous knowledge, madness, non-human influences on humanity, religion and superstition, fate and inevitability, and the risks associated with scientific discoveries.

### Body horror

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Body horror")]

Main article: [Body horror](/wiki/Body_horror "Body horror")

A genre that emerged in the 1970s, body horror films focus on the process of a bodily transformation. In these films, the body is either engulfed by some larger process or heading towards fragmentation and collapse.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJancovich199426-35)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJancovich1992112-36) The focus can be on apocalyptic implication of an entire society being overtaken, but the focus is generally upon an individual and their sense of identity, primarily them watching their own body change.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJancovich199426-35) The earliest appearance of the sub-genre was the work of director [David Cronenberg](/wiki/David_Cronenberg "David Cronenberg"), specifically with early films like *[Shivers](/wiki/Shivers_(1975_film) "Shivers (1975 film)")* (1975).[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJancovich199426-35)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJancovich1992112-36) Mark Jancovich of the University of Manchester declared that the transformation scenes in the genre provoke fear and repulsion, but also pleasure and excitement such as in *[The Thing](/wiki/The_Thing_(1982_film) "The Thing (1982 film)")* (1982) and *[The Fly](/wiki/The_Fly_(1986_film) "The Fly (1986 film)")* (1986).[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJancovich1992115-37)

### Christmas horror

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Christmas horror")]

Main article: [Christmas horror](/wiki/Christmas_horror "Christmas horror")

[Christmas horror](/wiki/Christmas_horror "Christmas horror") is a film subgenre that emerged in the 1970s with films such as *[Whoever Slew Auntie Roo?](/wiki/Whoever_Slew_Auntie_Roo%3F "Whoever Slew Auntie Roo?")* (1971) and *[Silent Night, Bloody Night](/wiki/Silent_Night,_Bloody_Night "Silent Night, Bloody Night")* (1972),[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewby2018-38) which were soon followed by the influential *[Black Christmas](/wiki/Black_Christmas_(1974_film) "Black Christmas (1974 film)")* (1974).[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewby2018-38)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUlaby2015-39) Defining the Christmas horror genre has been described as challenging,[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuPée20226-40) as it has generally been regarded as a sub-genre of the [slasher film](/wiki/Slasher_film "Slasher film").[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuPée20225-41)[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERockoff200330-42) Adam Rockoff, in *[Rue Morgue](/wiki/Rue_Morgue_(magazine) "Rue Morgue (magazine)")*, noted that the sub-genre sits within a trend of holiday-themed slasher films, alongside films such as *[My Bloody Valentine](/wiki/My_Bloody_Valentine_(film) "My Bloody Valentine (film)")* (1981) and *[April Fool's Day](/wiki/April_Fool%27s_Day_(1986_film) "April Fool's Day (1986 film)")* (1986).[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERockoff200330-42) Others take a broader view that Christmas horror is not limited to the slasher genre,[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuPée20225-41) noting how it evolved from the English Christmas tradition of telling ghost stories.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewby2018-38) Christmas in literature has historically included elements of "darkness"—fright, misery, death and decay—tracing its literary antecedents as far back as the biblical account of the [Massacre of the Innocents](/wiki/Massacre_of_the_Innocents "Massacre of the Innocents") and more recently in works such as E. T. A. Hoffmann's "[The Nutcracker and the Mouse King](/wiki/The_Nutcracker_and_the_Mouse_King "The Nutcracker and the Mouse King")" (1816) and Charles Dickens' *[A Christmas Carol](/wiki/A_Christmas_Carol "A Christmas Carol")* (1843).[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewby2018-38)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUlaby2015-39) Although ghosts have largely been replaced by serial killers, Christmas horror creates an outlet through which to explore "a modern reinvention of the Christmas ghost story".[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewby2018-38)

### Erotic horror

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Erotic horror")]

Main article: [Erotic horror](/wiki/Erotic_horror "Erotic horror")

[Erotic horror](/wiki/Erotic_horror "Erotic horror") is a subgenre of horror fiction that blends sensual and sexual imagery with horrific themes for the sake of [sexual arousal](/wiki/Sexual_arousal "Sexual arousal"). Erotic horror has had influences on French[[43]](#cite_note-www.kinoeye.org-43) and American horror cinema. The [works](/wiki/Jean_Rollin_filmography "Jean Rollin filmography") of [Jean Rollin](/wiki/Jean_Rollin "Jean Rollin"), such as *[Le Viol du Vampire](/wiki/Le_Viol_du_Vampire "Le Viol du Vampire")* and *[Fascination](/wiki/Fascination_(1979_film) "Fascination (1979 film)")*, are considered quintessential erotic horror films, blending deeply sexual imagery with gore.[[43]](#cite_note-www.kinoeye.org-43) American cinema has also featured notable erotic horror film franchises, such as *[Candyman](/wiki/Candyman_(film_series) "Candyman (film series)").*[[44]](#cite_note-44) An example of a British erotic horror film series is *[Hellraiser](/wiki/Hellraiser_(franchise) "Hellraiser (franchise)")*.[[45]](#cite_note-45) *[Alien](/wiki/Alien_(franchise) "Alien (franchise)")* features heavy erotic imagery, with the design of the [Xenomorph](/wiki/Xenomorph "Xenomorph") by [H. R. Giger](/wiki/H._R._Giger "H. R. Giger") featuring both [phallic](/wiki/Phallus "Phallus") and vaginal imagery, intended to symbolize patriarchal guilt[[46]](#cite_note-46) as well as sex, rape, and pregnancy.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### Extreme Horror

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Extreme Horror")]

[Extreme horror](/wiki/Extreme_cinema "Extreme cinema") is a subgenre of horror that emerged from early twentieth century theater through shocking performances from Paris, France's [Théâtre du Grand‑Guignol.](/wiki/Grand_Guignol "Grand Guignol")[[48]](#cite_note-48) The transition from theater to cinema allowed transgressive acts of uncensored sex, torture, mutilation, and [taboo](/wiki/Taboo "Taboo") exploitation to reach a wider audience. Notably, the 1970s herald in extreme horror that is frequently cited as the beginning of the film subgenre including *[Last House on the Left](/wiki/The_Last_House_on_the_Left "The Last House on the Left")* (1972), *[Salò, or the 120 Days of Sodom](/wiki/Sal%C3%B2,_or_the_120_Days_of_Sodom "Salò, or the 120 Days of Sodom")* (1975)*,* and *[I Spit on Your Grave](/wiki/I_Spit_on_Your_Grave "I Spit on Your Grave")* (1978)*.* [[49]](#cite_note-49) Extreme horror film series, such as *[Saw](/wiki/Saw_(franchise) "Saw (franchise)")* (2004) and [*Hostel*](/wiki/Hostel_(film_series) "Hostel (film series)") (2005), have reached mainstream media with character(s) appearances in video games namely *[Dead by Daylight](/wiki/Dead_by_Daylight "Dead by Daylight")* and the subject of musical lyrics for the band [Ice Nine Kills](/wiki/Ice_Nine_Kills "Ice Nine Kills").

### Folk horror

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Folk horror")]

Main article: [Folk horror](/wiki/Folk_horror "Folk horror")

[Folk horror](/wiki/Folk_horror "Folk horror") uses elements of [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore") or other [religious](/wiki/Religion "Religion") and cultural beliefs to instill fear in audiences. Folk horror films have featured [rural settings](/wiki/Rural_area "Rural area") and themes of isolation, religion and nature.[[50]](#cite_note-Hurley-2019-50)[[51]](#cite_note-Murphy-51) Frequently cited examples are *[Witchfinder General](/wiki/Witchfinder_General_(film) "Witchfinder General (film)")* (1968), *[The Blood on Satan's Claw](/wiki/The_Blood_on_Satan%27s_Claw "The Blood on Satan's Claw")* (1971), *[The Wicker Man](/wiki/The_Wicker_Man "The Wicker Man")* (1973), [*The Witch*](/wiki/The_Witch_(2015_film) "The Witch (2015 film)") (2015), and *[Midsommar](/wiki/Midsommar_(film) "Midsommar (film)")* (2019).[[50]](#cite_note-Hurley-2019-50)[[51]](#cite_note-Murphy-51) Local folklore and beliefs have been noted as being prevalent in horror films from the [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asian_cinema "Southeast Asian cinema") region, including [Thailand](/wiki/Thai_horror "Thai horror") and [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesian_horror "Indonesian horror").[[52]](#cite_note-Ferrarese-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Found footage horror

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Found footage horror")]

The [found footage horror](/wiki/Found_footage_(film_technique) "Found footage (film technique)") film "technique" gives the audience a [first person view](/wiki/First-person_narrative "First-person narrative") of the events on screen, and presents the footage as being discovered after. Horror films which are framed as being made up of "found-footage" merge the experiences of the audience and characters, which may induce suspense, shock, and bafflement.[[54]](#cite_note-54) [Alexandra Heller-Nicholas](/wiki/Alexandra_Heller-Nicholas "Alexandra Heller-Nicholas") noted that the popularity of sites like [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") in 2006 sparked a taste for amateur media, leading to the production of further films in the found footage horror genre later in the 2000s including the particularly financially successful *[Paranormal Activity](/wiki/Paranormal_Activity "Paranormal Activity")* (2007).[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeller-Nicholas20144-55)

### Gothic horror

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Gothic horror")]

Main article: [Gothic film](/wiki/Gothic_film "Gothic film")

In their book *Gothic film*, Richard J. McRoy and Richard J. Hand stated that "Gothic" can be argued as a very loose subgenre of horror, but argued that "Gothic" as a whole was a style like *[film noir](/wiki/Film_noir "Film noir")* and not bound to certain cinematic elements like the [Western](/wiki/Western_(genre)_film "Western (genre) film") or [science fiction film](/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film").[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandMcRoy20203-56) The term "gothic" is frequently used to describe a stylized approach to showcasing location, desire, and action in film. Contemporary views of the genre associate it with imagery of castles at hilltops and labyrinth like ancestral mansions that are in various states of disrepair.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandMcRoy20201-57) Narratives in these films often focus on an audience's fear and attraction to social change and rebellion.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandMcRoy20202-58) The genre can be applied to films as early as *The Haunted Castle* (1896), *Frankenstein* (1910) as well as to more complex iterations such as [Park Chan-wook](/wiki/Park_Chan-wook "Park Chan-wook")'s *[Stoker](/wiki/Stoker_(film) "Stoker (film)")* (2013) and [Jordan Peele](/wiki/Jordan_Peele "Jordan Peele")'s *[Get Out](/wiki/Get_Out_(film) "Get Out (film)")* (2017).[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandMcRoy20203-56)

The gothic style is applied to several films throughout the history of the horror film. This includes [Universal Pictures' horror films](/wiki/Universal_Classic_Monsters "Universal Classic Monsters") of the 1930s, the revival of gothic horror in the 1950s and 1960s with films from Hammer, [Roger Corman](/wiki/Roger_Corman "Roger Corman")'s Poe-cycle, and several Italian productions.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandMcRoy20205-6-59) By the 1970s American and British productions often had vampire films set in a contemporary setting, such as Hammer Films had their *Dracula* stories set in a modern setting and made other horror material which pushed the erotic content of their vampire films that was initiated by *Black Sunday*.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuir201210-60)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuir201211-61)[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurti201538-62) In the 1980s, the older horror characters of Dracula and Frankenstein's monster rarely appeared, with vampire themed films continued often in the tradition of authors like [Anne Rice](/wiki/Anne_Rice "Anne Rice") where vampirism becomes a lifestyle choice rather than plague or curse.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarriottNewman2018220-63) Following the release of [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola")'s *[Bram Stoker's Dracula](/wiki/Bram_Stoker%27s_Dracula_(1992_film) "Bram Stoker's Dracula (1992 film)")* (1992), a small wave of high-budgeted gothic horror romance films were released in the 1990s.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarriottNewman2018272-64)

### Natural horror

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Natural horror")]

See also: [List of natural horror films](/wiki/List_of_natural_horror_films "List of natural horror films")

Also described as "eco-horror", the [natural horror film](/wiki/Natural_horror_film "Natural horror film") is a subgenre "featuring nature running amok in the form of mutated beasts, carnivorous insects, and normally harmless animals or plants turned into cold-blooded killers."[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGregersdotterHoglundHallen201532-66) In 1963, [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock") defined a new genre nature taking revenge on humanity with *[The Birds](/wiki/The_Birds_(1963_film) "The Birds (1963 film)")* (1963) that was expanded into a trend into the 1970s. Following the success of *[Willard](/wiki/Willard_(1971_film) "Willard (1971 film)")* (1971), a film about killer rats, 1972 had similar films with *[Stanley](/wiki/Stanley_(1972_film) "Stanley (1972 film)")* (1972) and an official sequel *[Ben](/wiki/Ben_(1972_film) "Ben (1972 film)")* (1972).[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuir201217-67) Other films followed in suit such as *[Night of the Lepus](/wiki/Night_of_the_Lepus "Night of the Lepus")* (1972), *[Frogs](/wiki/Frogs_(film) "Frogs (film)")* (1972), *[Bug](/wiki/Bug_(1975_film) "Bug (1975 film)")* (1975), *[Squirm](/wiki/Squirm "Squirm")* (1976) and what Muir described as the "turning point" in the genre with *[Jaws](/wiki/Jaws_(film) "Jaws (film)")* (1975), which became the highest-grossing film at that point and moved the animal attacks genres "towards a less-fantastic route" with less giant animals and more real-life creatures such as *[Grizzly](/wiki/Grizzly_(1976_film) "Grizzly (1976 film)")* (1976) and *[Night Creature](/wiki/Night_Creature "Night Creature")* (1977), *[Orca](/wiki/Orca_(1977_film) "Orca (1977 film)")* (1977), and *[Jaws 2](/wiki/Jaws_2 "Jaws 2")* (1978).[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuir201217-67)[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuir201219-68)[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuir201220-69) The film is linked with the environmental movements that became more mainstream in the 1970s and early 1980s such [vegetarianism](/wiki/Vegetarianism "Vegetarianism"), [animal rights movements](/wiki/Animal_rights_movement "Animal rights movement"), and organizations such as [Greenpeace](/wiki/Greenpeace "Greenpeace").[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGregersdotterHoglundHallen201531-70) Following *Jaws*, sharks became the most popular animal of the genre, ranging from similar such as *[Mako: The Jaws of Death](/wiki/Mako:_The_Jaws_of_Death "Mako: The Jaws of Death")* (1976) and *[Great White](/wiki/Great_White_(1981_film) "Great White (1981 film)")* (1981) to the *[Sharknado](/wiki/Sharknado_(film_series) "Sharknado (film series)")* film series.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGregersdotterHoglundHallen201531-70) [James Marriott](/wiki/James_Marriott_(author) "James Marriott (author)") found that the genre had "lost momentum" since the 1970s while the films would still be made towards the turn of the millennium.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarriottNewman2018187-71)

### Psychological horror

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Psychological horror")]

Main article: [Psychological horror films](/wiki/Psychological_horror_films "Psychological horror films")

Bill Gibron of [PopMatters](/wiki/PopMatters "PopMatters") declared a mixed definition of the psychological horror film, ranging from definitions of anything that created a sense of disquiet or apprehension to a film where an audience's mind makes up what was not directly displayed visually. Gibron concluded it as a "clouded gray area between all out splatter and a trip through a cinematic dark ride."[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibron2013-72)

### Religious horror

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Religious horror")]

Main article: [Religious horror](/wiki/Religious_horror "Religious horror")

Religious horror is a subgenre of horror film whose common themes are based on religion and focus heavily on supernatural beings, often with [demons](/wiki/Demon "Demon") as the main antagonists that bring a sense of threat.[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74) Such films commonly use religious elements, including the crucifix or cross, holy water, the Bible, the rosary, the sign of the cross, the church, and prayer, which are forms of religious symbols and rituals used to depict the use of faith to defeat evil.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

### Slasher film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Slasher film")]

Main article: [Slasher film](/wiki/Slasher_film "Slasher film")

The [slasher film](/wiki/Slasher_film "Slasher film") is a horror subgenre which involves a killer murdering a group of people (often teenagers), usually by use of bladed tools.[[76]](#cite_note-:0p-76) In his book on the genre, author Adam Rockoff wrote that these villains represented a "rogue genre" of films that are "tough, problematic, and fiercely individualistic."[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuir200717-77) Following the financial success of *[Friday the 13th](/wiki/Friday_the_13th_(1980_film) "Friday the 13th (1980 film)")* (1980), at least 20 other slasher films appeared in 1980 alone.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarriottNewman2018218-78) These films usually revolved around three properties: unique social settings (campgrounds, schools, holidays) and a crime from the past committed (an accidental drowning, infidelity, a scorned lover) and a ready made group of victims (camp counselors, students, wedding parties).[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuir200721-79) The genre was derided by several contemporary film critics of the era such as Ebert, and often were highly profitable in the box office.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuir200718-19-80) The release of *[Scream](/wiki/Scream_(1996_film) "Scream (1996 film)")* (1996), led to a brief revival of the slasher films for the 1990s.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarriottNewman2018274-81) Other countries imitated the American slasher film revival, such as South Korea's early 2000s cycle with *[Bloody Beach](/wiki/Bloody_Beach "Bloody Beach")* (2000), *[Nightmare](/wiki/Nightmare_(2000_film) "Nightmare (2000 film)")* (2000) and *[The Record](/wiki/The_Record_(film) "The Record (film)")* (2000).[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChung201387-82)

### Splatter film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Splatter film")]

Main article: [Splatter film](/wiki/Splatter_film "Splatter film")

[Splatter](/wiki/Splatter_film "Splatter film") is a subgenre that depends on [violence](/wiki/Violence "Violence") and [gore](/wiki/Graphic_violence#Gore "Graphic violence") to display and accentuate a fascination with the theatricality of [mutilation](/wiki/Mutilation "Mutilation") and the vulnerability of the human body.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

### Supernatural horror

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Supernatural horror")]

Main article: [Supernatural horror film](/wiki/Supernatural_horror_film "Supernatural horror film")

[Supernatural horror films](/wiki/Supernatural_horror_film "Supernatural horror film"), sometimes referred to as [paranormal](/wiki/Paranormal "Paranormal") horror films, integrate [supernatural elements](/wiki/Supernatural_fiction "Supernatural fiction"), such as the [afterlife](/wiki/Afterlife "Afterlife"), [spirit possession](/wiki/Spirit_possession "Spirit possession") and religion into the horror genre.[[84]](#cite_note-84) Since they often focus on undead or non-physical beings, they may create unease in viewers without a visible presence on screen. This may include characters feeling a touch or chill in the air, furniture moving on its own, mysterious sounds, or lights flickering with no apparent cause. Prominent supernatural horror films include *[Paranormal Activity](/wiki/Paranormal_Activity "Paranormal Activity")* and *[The Conjuring](/wiki/The_Conjuring "The Conjuring")*.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

### Suburban gothic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Suburban gothic")]

Main article: [Suburban Gothic](/wiki/Suburban_Gothic "Suburban Gothic")

Suburban gothic is a subgenre of [Gothic fiction](/wiki/Gothic_fiction "Gothic fiction"), art, film and television, focused on anxieties associated with the creation of [suburban communities](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb"), particularly in the United States and the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"), from the 1950s and 1960s onwards.

This genre often explores themes of paranoia, conformity, and hidden dangers. Suburban horror films include *[Halloween](/wiki/Halloween_(1978_film) "Halloween (1978 film)")* (1978), *[A Nightmare on Elm Street](/wiki/A_Nightmare_on_Elm_Street "A Nightmare on Elm Street")* (1984), and *[The Stepford Wives](/wiki/The_Stepford_Wives_(1975_film) "The Stepford Wives (1975 film)")* (1975), all of which use the suburban setting to heighten the tension and fear.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[88]](#cite_note-88)

### Teen horror

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Teen horror")]

[Teen horror](/wiki/Teen_horror "Teen horror") is a horror subgenre that victimizes teenagers while usually promoting strong, [anti-conformity](/wiki/Anticonformity_(psychology) "Anticonformity (psychology)") teenage leads, appealing to young generations. This subgenre often depicts themes of sex, under-aged drinking, and gore.[[89]](#cite_note-89) Horror films aimed a young audience featuring teenage monsters grew popular in the 1950s with several productions from [American International Pictures](/wiki/American_International_Pictures "American International Pictures") (AIP) and productions of [Herman Cohen](/wiki/Herman_Cohen "Herman Cohen") with *[I Was a Teenage Werewolf](/wiki/I_Was_a_Teenage_Werewolf "I Was a Teenage Werewolf")* (1957) and *[I Was a Teenage Frankenstein](/wiki/I_Was_a_Teenage_Frankenstein "I Was a Teenage Frankenstein")* (1957).[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarriottNewman201870-90) This led to later productions like *[Daughter of Dr. Jekyll](/wiki/Daughter_of_Dr._Jekyll "Daughter of Dr. Jekyll")* (1957) and *[Frankenstein's Daughter](/wiki/Frankenstein%27s_Daughter "Frankenstein's Daughter")* (1958).[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarriottNewman201870-90) Teen horror cycle in the 1980s often showcased explicit gore and nudity, with John Kenneth Muir described as cautionary conservative tales where most of the films stated if you partook in such vices such as drugs or sex, your punishment of death would be handed out.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWest20184-91)
Prior to *Scream*, there were no popular teen horror films in the early 1990s.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShary200599-92) After the financial success of *Scream*, teen horror films became increasingly reflexive and self-aware until the end of the 1990s with films like *[I Know What You Did Last Summer](/wiki/I_Know_What_You_Did_Last_Summer "I Know What You Did Last Summer")* (1997) and non-slasher *[The Faculty](/wiki/The_Faculty "The Faculty")* (1998).[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShary2005102-93)[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShary200599-92) The genre lost prominence as teen films dealt with threats with more realism in films like *[Donnie Darko](/wiki/Donnie_Darko "Donnie Darko")* (2001) and *[Crazy/Beautiful](/wiki/Crazy/Beautiful "Crazy/Beautiful")* (2001).[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShary2005103-94) In her book on the 1990s teen horror cycle, Alexandra West described the general trend of these films is often looked down upon by critics, journals, and fans as being too glossy, trendy, and sleek to be considered worthwhile horror films.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWest20183–4-95)

## Regional horror films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Regional horror films")]

### Asian horror films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Asian horror films")]

Main article: [Horror films of Asia](/wiki/Horror_films_of_Asia "Horror films of Asia")

Horror films in [Asia](/wiki/Cinema_of_Asia "Cinema of Asia") have been noted as being inspired by national, cultural or religious [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore"), particularly beliefs in [ghosts](/wiki/Ghost "Ghost") or spirits.[[52]](#cite_note-Ferrarese-52)[[96]](#cite_note-Richards-2010-96) In *Asian Horror*, Andy Richards writes that there is a "widespread and engrained acceptance of [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural") forces" in many Asian cultures, and suggests this is related to [animist](/wiki/Animism "Animism"), [pantheist](/wiki/Pantheism "Pantheism") and [karmic](/wiki/Karma "Karma") religious traditions, as in [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Shintoism](/wiki/Shintoism "Shintoism").[[96]](#cite_note-Richards-2010-96) Although [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_horror_film "Chinese horror film"), [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_horror "Japanese horror"), [Thai](/wiki/Thai_horror "Thai horror") and [Korean](/wiki/Korean_horror "Korean horror") horror has arguably received the most international attention,[[96]](#cite_note-Richards-2010-96) horror also makes up a considerable proportion of [Cambodian](/wiki/Horror_films_of_Cambodia "Horror films of Cambodia")[[97]](#cite_note-97) and [Malaysian](/wiki/Malaysian_cinema "Malaysian cinema") cinema.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

### European horror films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: European horror films")]

Main article: [Horror films of Europe](/wiki/Horror_films_of_Europe "Horror films of Europe")

Ian Olney described the horror films of [Europe](/wiki/Cinema_of_Europe "Cinema of Europe") were often more erotic and "just plain stranger" than their British and American counter-parts.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlney2013xi-99) European horror films (generally referred to as Euro Horror)[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWynter201644-100) draw from distinctly European cultural sources, including [surrealism](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism"), [romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism"), [decadent tradition](/wiki/Decadent_movement "Decadent movement"), early 20th century [pulp-literature](/wiki/Pulp_magazine "Pulp magazine"), [film serials](/wiki/Film_serials "Film serials"), and [erotic comics](/wiki/Erotic_comics "Erotic comics").[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlney20136-101) In comparison to the narrative logic in American genre films, these films focused on imagery, excessiveness, and the irrational.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlney20137-102)

Between the mid-1950s and the mid-1980s, European horror films emerged from countries like Italy, Spain and France, and were shown in the United States predominantly at drive-in theatre and [grindhouse](/wiki/Grindhouse "Grindhouse") theaters.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlney2013xi-99) As producers and distributors all over the world were interested in horror films, regardless of their origin, changes started occurring in European low-budget filmmaking that allowed for productions in the 1960s and 1970s for horror films from Italy, France, Germany, the United Kingdom and Spain, as well as co-productions between these countries.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELazaro-Reboll201211-12-103) Several productions, such as those in Italy, were co-productions due to the lack of international stars within the country.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeffernan2004141-104) European horror films began developing strong [cult following](/wiki/Cult_following "Cult following") since the late 1990s.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlney2013xi-99)

### Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Oceania")]

#### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Australia")]

It is unknown when [Australia's cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_Australia "Cinema of Australia") first horror title may have been, with thoughts ranging from *[The Strangler's Grip](/wiki/The_Strangler%27s_Grip "The Strangler's Grip")* (1912) to *[The Face at the Window](/wiki/The_Face_at_the_Window_(1919_film) "The Face at the Window (1919 film)")* (1919) while stories featuring ghosts would appear in *[Guyra Ghost Mystery](/wiki/The_Guyra_Ghost_Mystery "The Guyra Ghost Mystery")* (1921).[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShelley20129-105) By 1913, the more prolific era of Australian cinema ended with production not returning with heavy input of government finance in the 1970s.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShelley20125-6-106) It took until the 1970s for Australia to develop sound film with television films that eventually received theatrical release with *[Dead Easy](/wiki/Dead_Easy_(1970_film) "Dead Easy (1970 film)")* (1970) and *[Night of Fear](/wiki/Night_of_Fear_(film) "Night of Fear (film)")* (1973). *[The Cars That Ate Paris](/wiki/The_Cars_That_Ate_Paris "The Cars That Ate Paris")* (1974) was the first Australian horror production made for theatrical release.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShelley20129-105) 1970s Australian art cinema was funded by state film corporations, who considered them more culturally acceptable than local exploitation films ([Ozploitation](/wiki/Ozploitation "Ozploitation")), which was part of the Australian phenomenon called the [cultural cringe](/wiki/Cultural_cringe "Cultural cringe").[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShelley201210-107) The greater success of genre films like *[Mad Max](/wiki/Mad_Max_(film) "Mad Max (film)")* (1979), *[The Last Wave](/wiki/The_Last_Wave "The Last Wave")* (1977) and *[Patrick](/wiki/Patrick_(1978_film) "Patrick (1978 film)")* (1978) led to the [Australian Film Commission](/wiki/Australian_Film_Commission "Australian Film Commission") to change its focus to being a more commercial operation. This closed in 1980 as its funding was abused by investors using them as tax avoiding measures. A new development known as the [10BA tax shelter scheme](/wiki/Film_and_television_financing_in_Australia "Film and television financing in Australia") was developed ushering a slew of productions, leading to what Peter Shelley, author of *Australian Horror Films*, suggested meant "making a profit was more important than making a good film."[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShelley201210-107) Shelley called these films derivative of "American films and presenting generic American material".[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShelley201210-107) These films included the horror film productions of [Antony I. Ginnane](/wiki/Antony_I._Ginnane "Antony I. Ginnane").[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOdellLe_Blanc2007192-108) While Australia would have success with international films between the mid-1980s and the 2000s, less than five horror films were produced in the country between 1993 and 2000.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShelley201212-109)[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWithers2016-110) It was only after the success of *[Wolf Creek](/wiki/Wolf_Creek_(film) "Wolf Creek (film)")* (2005) that a new generation of filmmakers would continuously make horror genre films in Australia that continued into the 2010s.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShelley201212-109)[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWithers2016-110)

#### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

By 2005, [New Zealand](/wiki/Cinema_of_New_Zealand "Cinema of New Zealand") has produced around 190 feature films, with about 88% of them being made after 1976.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConrich2005115-111) New Zealand horror film history was described by Philip Matthews of [Stuff](/wiki/Stuff_(website) "Stuff (website)") as making "po-faced gothic and now we do horror for laughs."[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthews2017-112) Among the earliest known New Zealand horror films productions are *[Strange Behavior](/wiki/Strange_Behavior "Strange Behavior")* (1981), a co-production with Australia and *[Death Warmed Up](/wiki/Death_Warmed_Up "Death Warmed Up")* (1984) a single production.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConrich2005117-113) Early features such as [Melanie Read](/wiki/Melanie_Read "Melanie Read")'s *[Trial Run](/wiki/Trial_Run_(1984_film) "Trial Run (1984 film)")* (1984) where a mother is sent to remote cottage to photograph penguins and finds it habitat to haunted spirits, and Gaylene Preston's *[Mr. Wrong](/w/index.php?title=Mr._Wrong_(1984_film)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mr. Wrong (1984 film) (page does not exist)")* (1984) purchases a car that is haunted by its previous owner.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConrich2005116-114) Other films imitate American slasher and splatter films with *[Bridge to Nowhere](/wiki/Bridge_to_Nowhere_(film) "Bridge to Nowhere (film)")* (1986), and the early films of [Peter Jackson](/wiki/Peter_Jackson "Peter Jackson") who combined splatter films with comedy with *[Bad Taste](/wiki/Bad_Taste "Bad Taste")* (1988) and *[Braindead](/wiki/Braindead_(film) "Braindead (film)")* (1992) which has the largest following of the mentioned films.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConrich2005117-113) Film producer [Ant Timpson](/wiki/Ant_Timpson "Ant Timpson") had an influence curating New Zealand horror films, creating the Incredibly Strange Film Festival in the 1990s and producing his own horror films over the 2010s including *[The ABCs of Death](/wiki/The_ABCs_of_Death "The ABCs of Death")* (2012), *[Deathgasm](/wiki/Deathgasm "Deathgasm")* (2015), and *[Housebound](/wiki/Housebound_(2014_film) "Housebound (2014 film)")* (2014).[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthews2017-112) Timpson noted the latter horror entries from New Zealand are all humorous films like *[What We Do in the Shadows](/wiki/What_We_Do_in_the_Shadows "What We Do in the Shadows")* (2014) with [Jonathan King](/wiki/Jonathan_King_(film_director) "Jonathan King (film director)"), director of *[Black Sheep](/wiki/Black_Sheep_(2006_New_Zealand_film) "Black Sheep (2006 New Zealand film)")* (2006) and *[The Tattooist](/wiki/The_Tattooist "The Tattooist")* (2007) stating "I'd love to see a genuinely scary New Zealand film but I don't know if New Zealand audiences – or the funding bodies – are keen."[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthews2017-112)

### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: North America")]

#### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Mexico")]

Main article: [Horror films of Mexico](/wiki/Horror_films_of_Mexico "Horror films of Mexico")

After the 1931 release of an American-produced Spanish-language version of *[Dracula](/wiki/Dracula_(1931_Spanish-language_film) "Dracula (1931 Spanish-language film)")* by [George Melford](/wiki/George_Melford "George Melford") for the Latin-American market employing Mexican actors, Mexican horror films were produced throughout the 1930s and 1940s, often reflecting on the overarching theme of [science vs. religion conflict](/wiki/Relationship_between_religion_and_science "Relationship between religion and science").[[115]](#cite_note-115) Ushered by the release of *[El vampiro](/wiki/El_vampiro "El vampiro")*, the Mexploitation horror film era started in 1957, with films characterised by their low production values and camp appeal, often featuring vampires, wrestlers, and Aztec mummies.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreen20056–9-116) A key figure in the Mexican horror scene (particularly in [Germán Robles](/wiki/Germ%C3%A1n_Robles "Germán Robles")-starred vampire films) was producer [Abel Salazar](/wiki/Abel_Salazar_(actor) "Abel Salazar (actor)").[[117]](#cite_note-117) The late 1960s saw the advent of the prominence of [Carlos Enrique Taboada](/wiki/Carlos_Enrique_Taboada "Carlos Enrique Taboada") as an standout Mexican horror filmmaker, with films such as *[Hasta el viento tiene miedo](/wiki/Hasta_el_viento_tiene_miedo "Hasta el viento tiene miedo")* (1967), *[El libro de piedra](/wiki/El_libro_de_piedra "El libro de piedra")* (1968), *[Más negro que la noche](/wiki/M%C3%A1s_negro_que_la_noche "Más negro que la noche")* (1975) or *[Veneno para las hadas](/wiki/Veneno_para_las_hadas "Veneno para las hadas")* (1984).[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarcía_Ruiz201941–42-118) Mexican horror cinema has been noted for the mashup of classic gothic and romantic themes and characters with autochthonous features of the Mexican culture such as the [Ranchería](/wiki/Rancher%C3%ADa "Ranchería") setting, the colonial past or the myth of [La Llorona](/wiki/La_Llorona "La Llorona") (shared with other Hispanic-American nations).[[119]](#cite_note-119)

Horror has proven to be a dependable genre at the Mexican box office in the 21st-century, with Mexico ranking as having the world's largest relative popularity of the genre among viewers (ahead of South Korea), according to a 2016 research.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

## Effects on audiences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Effects on audiences")]

### Psychological effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Psychological effects")]

In a study done by Uri Hasson et al., brain waves were observed via [functional magnetic resonance imaging](/wiki/Functional_magnetic_resonance_imaging "Functional magnetic resonance imaging") (fMRI). This study used the inter-subject correlation analysis (ISC) method of determining results. It was shown that audience members tend to focus on certain facets in a particular scene simultaneously and tend to sit as still as possible while watching horror films.[[121]](#cite_note-121)

In another study done by John Greene and Glenn Sparks, it was found that the audience tends to experience the [excitation transfer process](/wiki/Excitation-transfer_theory "Excitation-transfer theory") (ETP) which causes a physiological arousal in audience members. The ETP refers to the feelings experienced immediately after an emotion-arousing experience, such as watching a horror film. In this case, audience members' heart rate, blood pressure and respiration all increased while watching films with violence. Audience members with positive feedback regarding the horror film have feelings similar to happiness or joy felt with friends, but intensified. Alternatively, audience members with negative feedback regarding the film would typically feel emotions they would normally associate with negative experiences in their life.[[122]](#cite_note-122)

Only about 10% of the American population enjoy the physiological rush felt immediately after watching horror films.[[123]](#cite_note-123) The population that does not enjoy horror films could experience emotional fallout similar to that of [PTSD](/wiki/Post-traumatic_stress_disorder "Post-traumatic stress disorder") if the environment reminds them of particular scenes.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

A 2021 study suggested horror films that explore [grief](/wiki/Grief "Grief") can provide psychological benefits to the bereaved, with the genre well suited to representing grief through its genre conventions.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

### Physical effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Physical effects")]

In a study by Medes et al., prolonged exposure to [infrasound](/wiki/Infrasound "Infrasound") and low-frequency noise (<500 Hz) in long durations has an effect on vocal range (i.e. longer exposure tends to form a lower phonation frequency range).[[126]](#cite_note-126) Another study by Baliatsas et al. observed that there is a correlation between exposure to infrasound and low-frequency noises and sleep-related problems.[[127]](#cite_note-127) Though most horror films keep the audio around 20–30 Hz, the noise can still be unsettling in long durations.[[17]](#cite_note-Ranker-17)

Another technique used in horror films to provoke a response from the audience is [cognitive dissonance](/wiki/Cognitive_dissonance "Cognitive dissonance"), which is when someone experiences tension in themselves and is urged to relieve that tension.[[128]](#cite_note-128) Dissonance is the clashing of unpleasant or harsh sounds.[[129]](#cite_note-129) A study by Prete et al. identified that the ability to recognize dissonance relied on the left hemisphere of the brain, while consonance relied on the right half.[[130]](#cite_note-Prete-2015-130) There is a stronger preference for consonance; this difference is noticeable even in early stages of life.[[130]](#cite_note-Prete-2015-130) Previous musical experience also can influence a dislike for dissonance.[[130]](#cite_note-Prete-2015-130)

[Skin conductance responses](/wiki/Skin_conductance_response "Skin conductance response") (SCRs), heart rate (HR), and [electromyographic](/wiki/Electromyography "Electromyography") (EMG) responses vary in response to emotional stimuli, showing higher for [negative emotions](/wiki/Negative_emotion "Negative emotion") in what is known as the "negative bias."[[131]](#cite_note-Dellacherie-2011-131) When applied to dissonant music, HR decreases (as a bodily form of adaptation to harsh stimulation), SCR increases, and EMG responses in the face are higher.[[131]](#cite_note-Dellacherie-2011-131) The typical reactions go through a two-step process of first orienting to the problem (the slowing of HR), then a defensive process (a stronger increase in SCR and an increase in HR).[[131]](#cite_note-Dellacherie-2011-131) This initial response can sometimes result in a [fight-or-flight response](/wiki/Fight-or-flight_response "Fight-or-flight response"), which is the characteristic of dissonance that horror films rely on to frighten and unsettle viewers.[[17]](#cite_note-Ranker-17)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Reception")]

### In film criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: In film criticism")]

See also: [Vulgar auteurism](/wiki/Vulgar_auteurism "Vulgar auteurism")

Critic [Robin Wood](/wiki/Robin_Wood_(critic) "Robin Wood (critic)") was not the first film critic to take the horror film seriously, but his article *Return of the Repressed* in 1978 helped inaugurate the horror film into academic study as a genre.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeirseMartin20137-132) Wood later stated that he was surprised that his work, as well as the writing of Richard Lippe and Andrew Britton would receive "historic importance" intellectual views of the film genre.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeirseMartin20137-132) William Paul in his book *Laughing Screaming* comments that "the negative definition of the lower works would have it that they are less subtle than higher genres. More positively, it could be said that they are more direct. Where lower forms are explicit, higher forms tend to operate more by indirection. Because of this indirection the higher forms are often regarded as being more metaphorical, and consequently more resonant, more open to the exegetical analyses of the academic industry."[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaul199432-133)

Steffen Hantke noted that academic criticism about horror cinema had "always operated under duress" noting that challenges in legitimizing its subject, finding "career-minded academics might have always suspected that they were studying something that was ultimately too frivolous, garish, and sensationalistic to warrant serious critical attention".[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHantke2007-134)

Some commentary has suggested that horror films have been underrepresented or underappreciated as serious works worthy of [film criticism](/wiki/Film_criticism "Film criticism") and major films awards.[[135]](#cite_note-135)[[136]](#cite_note-136) As of 2025, only seven horror films have been nominated for the [Academy Award for Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture "Academy Award for Best Picture"), with [*The Silence of the Lambs*](/wiki/The_Silence_of_the_Lambs_(film) "The Silence of the Lambs (film)") being the sole winner.[[137]](#cite_note-137) However, horror films have still won major awards.[[138]](#cite_note-138)

Critics have also commented on the [representation of women](/wiki/Gender_in_horror_films "Gender in horror films")[[139]](#cite_note-139)[[140]](#cite_note-140)[[141]](#cite_note-Spines-141)[[142]](#cite_note-Nowell-142) and [disability](/wiki/Disability_in_horror_films "Disability in horror films")[[143]](#cite_note-143) in horror films, as well as the [prevalence of racial stereotypes](/wiki/Race_in_horror_films "Race in horror films").[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145)

### Censorship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Censorship")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Horror_film&action=edit&section=). *(January 2022)* |

Many horror films have been the subject of [moral panic](/wiki/Moral_panic "Moral panic"), [censorship](/wiki/Film_censorship "Film censorship") and legal controversy.

In the United Kingdom, [film censorship](/wiki/Film_censorship_in_the_United_Kingdom "Film censorship in the United Kingdom") has frequently been applied to horror films.[[146]](#cite_note-146) A moral panic over several [slasher](/wiki/Slasher_film "Slasher film") films in the 1980s led to many of them being banned but released on videotape; the phenomenon became popularly termed "[video nasties](/wiki/Video_nasty "Video nasty")".[[147]](#cite_note-147)[[148]](#cite_note-148) Constraints on permitted subject matter in [Indonesian films](/wiki/Cinema_of_Indonesia "Cinema of Indonesia") has also influenced [Indonesian horror films](/wiki/Indonesian_horror "Indonesian horror").[[149]](#cite_note-149) In March 2008, [China banned](/wiki/Film_censorship_in_China "Film censorship in China") all horror films from its market.[[150]](#cite_note-150)

In the U.S., the [Motion Picture Production Code](/wiki/Motion_Picture_Production_Code "Motion Picture Production Code") which was implemented in 1930, set moral guidelines for film content, restraining movies containing controversial themes, graphic violence, explicit sexuality and/or nudity. The gradual abandonment of the Code, and its eventual formal repeal in 1968 (when it was replaced by the [MPAA film rating system](/wiki/Motion_Picture_Association_of_America_film_rating_system "Motion Picture Association of America film rating system"))[[151]](#cite_note-151) offered more freedom to the movie industry.
